title: Versioning, Delete Markers, Object Lock, and Recovery · Go Semantics Case: Worm Not Backup
summary: A team enables versioning after an incident, assumes delete markers are backups, and applies compliance retention without testing irreversible governance and restore procedures. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the worm not backup increment in version and immutable-retention recovery runbook. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and eviden…
objectives: Separate WORM retention, version recovery, replication, independent backup, restore testing, and account-compromise boundaries.
Apply governance, compliance, legal hold, retention date, and authorized bypass by version with irreversible-risk review.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Worm Not Backup · Theory Worm Not Backup
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 4311 during an SDK and infrastructure canary. Build deterministic Go evidence to validate version identity, immutable retention meaning, and executed restore evidence; do not treat an ETag, CORS result, emulator, or successful SDK call as stronger proof than it is; then introduce a duplicate event or ambiguous response and reconcile without duplicate effect. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Worm Not Backup contract for the supplied data.
